svg.title: Proving Languages Not to be Regular

section.text: Proving Languages Not to be Regular
In the theory of computation, regular languages are a fundamental concept.
The pumping lemma states that if a language is regular, then there exists a pumping length p such that any string w in the language with length at least p can be divided into three parts w = xyz.
- |xy| ≤ p
- For all i ≥ 0, xy^iz is in the language
This language is not regular.
To prove it, we can use the pumping lemma.
Assume that L is regular, and let p be the pumping length.
Consider the string w = a^pb^p.
Since |w| ≥ p, we can divide w into three parts w = xyz.
Since |y| > 0, y must contain at least one a.
Let y = a^k for some k > 0.
Then xy^2z = a^(p+k)b^p, which is not in L.
This contradicts the pumping lemma, so L is not regular.

section.data-narration: Example 2: The Language L = {ww | w ∈ {a, b}^*}
section.text: This language is also not regular.
To prove it, we can use the pumping lemma.
Assume that L is regular, and let p be the pumping length.
Consider the string w = (ab)^p(ab)^p.
Since |w| ≥ p, we can divide w into three parts w = xyz.
Since |y| > 0, y must contain at least one a or b.
Let y = a^k b^l for some k, l ≥ 0.
Then xy^2z = (ab)^p(ab)^{k+l}(ab)^p, which is not in L.
This contradicts the pumping lemma, so L is not regular.
